evidence_quotes.quote: Payment processors should not have the power to decide what a customer can buy with their money, as long as what he is buying is legal.
They threaten to remove their payment systems if they do not dispose of such products.

paragraphs.text: Payment processors such as Visa and Mastercard have been pushing companies such as Steam and Itch.io to remove products from their libraries and even customers’ accounts, simply because these payment processors consider them inappropriate, even if they are completely legal in most countries. They threaten to remove their payment systems if they do not dispose of such products. All this happened after an Australian group called ‘collective shout’ was disturbing them. This meant that they were influenced by a small group in a single country to ban and censor global media. This is not a precedent that I believe we want to repeat, especially considering that the same group seems to have been openly anti-abortion and anti-LGBTQ +. This gives rise to serious concerns about mono- and over-power behaviour, where these companies may demand some censorship in games or other media. This time it could have been a lot of content for adults, but in the future they could put pressure on a platform to remove content with LGBTQ + topics or critical media from a politically sensitive individual or topic or topics related to a minority, they could even prohibit the free use of some colour. This is due to scientifically reviewed and unfounded complaints created by a local group. This time it has been the capriad of some people, but if the company uses that power to limit certain content or transactions for its benefit. Payment processors should not have the power to decide what a customer can buy with their money, as long as what he is buying is legal.
